quote: solar shingles. And Yeah. I was does that so do you guys do that?
stewart_read: Eric is not a cold lead. He has researched solar product categories, identified a specific product (solar shingles / integrated roof tiles), and is asking a direct qualification question. This is a buyer signal for a future purchase, not a casual inquiry. The enthusiasm is real — it just points at a future home, not the current address.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to show Bucky what a qualified future-pipeline lead sounds like. Eric's product specificity (solar shingles) and forward-looking framing ('my next house') are the signals that should have triggered a pivot from current-home pitch to future-home capture. The question to ask Bucky in the 1-on-1: 'What would you have done differently if you knew this customer was going to buy solar in two years for a new house?'

quote: So those they're we're, like, we have those on order, but they don't come out for, like, another couple years.
stewart_read: Solar shingles (integrated roof tiles) are explicitly listed under ion_does_NOT_sell: tesla_solar_roof_tiles. Bucky's answer — 'we have those on order, couple years out' — is factually incorrect per the codex; Ion does not carry and is not receiving Tesla solar roof tiles. However, the honest direction of the answer (we don't have them yet) is correct. The gap is not a scope-creep failure — it is a product-knowledge gap that also missed the bridge: Ion does install its own solar panels on new construction, and the closer conversation is where product options get laid out. Bucky had an opening to say 'we don't do the integrated shingle product, but our panels work on new builds — let me get you to a specialist who can show you what that looks like for your future home.'
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky was being honest about a product limitation and chose not to oversell. This is partially correct — the honesty is right. But the missing bridge to Ion's actual panel offering and new-construction capability means the honest answer became a dead end rather than a redirect.
coaching_implication: Two things to cover with Bucky: (1) The correct product fact — Ion does not sell Tesla solar roof tiles and is not receiving them on order; that framing could create a false expectation. (2) The bridge move — when a customer asks about a product Ion doesn't carry, the response is 'we don't do that specific product, but here's what we do offer that might serve your situation.' Manager should confirm with Kenny what Ion's new-construction panel offering looks like so Bucky has accurate language.

quote: I'm probably looking to move probably in about three years, so I don't necessarily wanna do that investment on that right at this point in time.
stewart_read: This is a flip opportunity that Bucky walked past. Eric has just handed Bucky a time-anchored objection: 'three years, not worth the investment.' The inversion available here is not about the current house — it is about the future house. Eric is telling Bucky he is planning a significant housing move and is already thinking about solar for the next property. The flip is not 'you should do it on this house anyway' (which is what Bucky attempted) — it is 'three years is exactly the right time to start understanding what you want, so you can build it into the new home from day one.'
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky heard 'three years / not worth it' as a standard timeline objection and deployed the bill-swap reframe. This is the reactive hypothesis — it treats Eric's statement as an objection to the current-home pitch rather than as a signal about his actual buying horizon.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the flip structure from coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion and apply it to this specific moment. The suggested reframe direction (not verbatim): 'Three years is actually perfect timing — if you're building or buying new, you want to know what solar looks like before you sign on the house, not after. That's exactly what our specialist conversation is for.' This is the move that converts a dead-end call into a future-pipeline sit.

quote: I've I've looked at that. I know that you guys people offer that. But talked with my realtor, and he advised me not to do that if I'm looking to sell in the near future
stewart_read: Eric is not changing his mind — he never had a mind to change on the current house. He is citing a trusted third-party advisor (his realtor) who has already counseled against the bill-swap path. This is not a rebuttals.changed_mind scenario. It is a third-party authority objection with no direct codex rebuttal. Bucky's response ('Okay. Yeah. And you know about the installation and all that? No cost?') attempts to add a new data point (no-cost installation) but does not acknowledge the realtor's authority or engage with Eric's actual concern. The realtor's advice is reasonable and Eric has internalized it — arguing around it without acknowledging it reads as dismissive.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky was trying to introduce a new angle (no-cost installation) to create a crack in the realtor's advice. This is partially strategic, but the execution skips the acknowledgment step — jumping to a counter-data-point without validating the advisor's credibility first makes the move feel like a brush-off rather than a genuine engagement.
coaching_implication: Flag this as a schema gap for Kenny: Ion does not have a documented rebuttal for third-party advisor authority (realtor, financial advisor, adult child). The correct move here is acknowledge-then-redirect, not counter-data-point. Suggested direction for the 1-on-1: 'Your realtor knows your market — that's smart to check. The question I'd want to answer for you is what solar looks like on the next house, where you're planning to stay long-term. That's a different conversation.' This keeps the realtor's credibility intact and pivots to the real opportunity.

quote: I'm aware yeah. I'm aware of all that. It's kind of some of the paperwork and some of the behind the scenes for making quick sales and stuff like that.
stewart_read: Eric is not a skeptic — he is an informed buyer. He knows about no-cost installation, has researched the bill-swap model, understands the paperwork implications for home sales, and has consulted a realtor. This level of category knowledge is a strong qualification signal for a future purchase. Bucky does not register this sophistication; he continues with a generic pitch rather than acknowledging Eric as a peer-level researcher and pivoting to the future-home conversation Eric has been signaling throughout.
coaching_implication: Use this moment in the 1-on-1 to show Bucky what an informed future buyer sounds like. The coaching question: 'When a customer tells you they already know how the product works, what does that tell you about them?' The answer Bucky should arrive at: this customer is not a skeptic to overcome — he is a buyer to capture for the right timing. The verify.interest_in_solar item is the emotional anchor Bucky never pulled: 'You clearly know this space — what's drawing you to solar for the new place specifically?'

quote: I mean, I just But you I'm not gonna lie. I wanna see what we could do for you because, like, your home is literally perfect for solar, and, it's, like, all the right conditions.
stewart_read: Bucky is selling the current house's solar suitability to a customer who has said three times he is not interested in the current house. 'Your home is literally perfect for solar' is a features-and-conditions pitch aimed at the wrong target. It also has a slightly pressured tone ('I'm not gonna lie, I wanna see what we could do for you') that reads as rep-need rather than customer-benefit. Eric has been clear and consistent; Bucky is not hearing him.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky is making one final attempt to create curiosity before accepting the no. This is the more charitable read, but the execution fails because it pitches the wrong house and uses rep-need framing rather than customer-curiosity framing.
coaching_implication: This is the moment to show Bucky the difference between persistence and misdirection. Persistence on the right target (future home) would have been valuable here. Persistence on the wrong target (current house) after three clear signals is the failure mode. The coaching question: 'What was Eric actually interested in on this call?' If Bucky can answer that correctly, he can see why this line missed.

quote: It really was gonna be for what my next house is gonna be. And so at this point in time, I'd probably say I probably don't wanna go any further then.
stewart_read: Eric states his actual interest for the fourth time — solar for a future home — and signals he wants to end the call. This is the last moment where Bucky could have captured pipeline value: ask about the new home's timeline, location, whether it's new construction or existing, and offer to reconnect when Eric is closer to the purchase. None of this happens. Bucky accepts the close-out and says goodbye. A qualified, informed, motivated future buyer exits with no follow-up mechanism and no reason to call Ion when the time comes.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky accepted the close-out gracefully to preserve goodwill for a future touch. This would be strategic if Bucky had captured any information to enable that future touch — a timeline, a location, a callback preference. He did not, so the graceful exit produces no pipeline value.
coaching_implication: The pipeline-capture move Bucky needed here: 'Totally understand — when you're getting closer to that purchase, what's the best way to reach you? I can make a note to follow up around that timeline.' This is not a booking attempt — it is a future-pipeline flag. Manager should work with Bucky on recognizing when the right outcome is not a sit today but a pipeline capture for a future sit. This also surfaces a schema gap: Ion's codex does not currently document a future-pipeline capture protocol for leads who are pre-purchase on a new home.

stewart_read: Bucky's close-out is clean and professional — no pressure, no guilt, no awkward lingering. This is worth noting as a positive: Bucky did not damage the relationship. The failure is not in how he ended the call but in what he failed to do before ending it. The call's shape is energy_leaked, but the leak was gradual and the exit was handled with appropriate grace.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge to Bucky that the close-out was handled well — no pressure, no damage to the relationship. Then use that as the setup for the harder coaching question: 'You left on good terms. What would it have taken to leave on good terms AND with a reason to call Eric back in 18 months?' This frames the coaching as addition, not correction.
